employment_history.description_of_duties_responsibilities_c: Supervise a team of 12 security officers responsible for 24/7 coverage of hospital facilities, including emergency department, main entrances, parking structures, and restricted clinical areas; create and maintain weekly schedules, approve timecards, and coordinate coverage for special events such as high‑profile patient visits or community health fairs.

Conduct incident response and preliminary investigations for disturbances, missing property, workplace violence concerns, and safety violations; write detailed incident reports using the hospital’s reporting system; review security camera footage; and coordinate with Kalamazoo Department of Public Safety officers when situations escalate beyond internal capabilities.

Lead annual training for new security staff on de‑escalation, use of force continuum, radio communication protocols, report writing, and HIPAA‑sensitive interactions with patients and families; assisted in revising the hospital’s active shooter and lockdown procedures in 2021, and participated in multiple full‑scale emergency drills with local law enforcement and fire services.
employment_history.current_employers_name_2: Kalamazoo Community College Public Safety Department
employment_history.description_of_duties_responsibilities_c: Patrolled campus buildings, parking lots, and surrounding grounds on foot and by vehicle to deter crime, enforce campus regulations, and provide visible safety presence; responded to calls for service including medical assists, minor traffic accidents, suspicious persons, and noise or disturbance complaints from students and staff.

Assisted with traffic and crowd control during large campus events such as commencement, athletic events, and visiting speaker series; issued campus citations for parking violations and policy infractions; completed clear, detailed daily activity logs and incident reports used for internal records and, when necessary, by partnering law enforcement agencies.

Completed in‑service trainings on crisis intervention, Title IX awareness, and Clery Act reporting requirements; frequently served as the primary contact for students in emotional distress, referring them to counseling or other support services; commended in 2017 for calmly managing a vehicle fire in a campus lot by coordinating evacuation, contacting fire services, and preserving a safe perimeter until the scene was cleared.

Provided customer service in a busy neighborhood hardware store, assisting homeowners and contractors with selecting tools, fasteners, paints, and building materials; developed strong working knowledge of basic plumbing, electrical, and carpentry supplies in order to give accurate, practical advice to customers.

Handled opening and closing procedures as a shift lead, including counting drawers, preparing daily deposits, securing the building, and verifying that all doors, windows, and loading areas were locked and alarmed; trained new hires on point‑of‑sale systems, inventory procedures, and customer‑service expectations.

Assisted with weekly inventory counts and loss‑prevention efforts; helped implement a basic camera and mirror layout that reduced small‑item theft near the front registers; consistently recognized by the owner for reliability, punctuality, and willingness to cover shifts at short notice during peak spring and summer seasons.

special_training_experience_skills_or_ab.special_training_experience_skills_or_ab: I have completed multiple law‑enforcement‑adjacent trainings that I believe would be directly valuable to the Kalamazoo County Sheriff’s Office. These include a 40‑hour Crisis Intervention Team (CIT) course hosted by the Kalamazoo Department of Public Safety, where I learned structured techniques for de‑escalating encounters with individuals experiencing mental health crises, substance‑use issues, or developmental disabilities. In my current role at the medical center, I have applied these skills numerous times—for example, by calmly talking down an agitated visitor in the emergency department waiting room who was threatening staff, using active listening, clear boundaries, and collaboration with nursing personnel to resolve the situation without physical intervention.

I also hold current certifications in Basic Life Support (BLS) and CPR/AED for Healthcare Providers through the American Heart Association, which I renew every two years. On several occasions I have been the first person on scene for medical emergencies in public areas of the hospital campus and at my previous college employer, including a diabetic emergency in a parking structure and a suspected opioid overdose in a campus restroom. In both cases I was able to quickly assess the scene for safety, contact EMS, provide basic care within my training, and relay clear information to responding paramedics. Additionally, I have completed institutional trainings in workplace violence prevention, active‑shooter response (Run–Hide–Fight model and hospital‑specific lockdown procedures), and incident command structure, and I am very comfortable using radio communications, CCTV systems, and electronic access‑control platforms.

Beyond formal training, I bring strong written and verbal communication skills, attention to detail, and familiarity with report writing that meets evidentiary standards. I routinely draft incident reports that have later been referenced by law enforcement and legal departments, so I am diligent about including times, locations, witness statements, and objective descriptions of behavior rather than assumptions. I am fluent in English and conversational in Spanish, which has been helpful when interacting with Spanish‑speaking patients’ families and campus visitors; I often act as an informal bridge until a certified interpreter is available. I am comfortable working nights, weekends, and holidays, and I have extensive experience in high‑stress environments where remaining calm, respectful, and professional is essential—qualities that I believe align closely with the expectations and culture of the Sheriff’s Office.
